items.title: Admin dashboard
items.description: An admin dashboard is the landing surface that summarizes system state through a small grid of KPI cards (counts, pending-approval totals, recent activity) plus one or two trend or distribution panels. It must render from cheap, indexed aggregate queries and a cached service-health snapshot so it loads in well under a second even on an empty or seeded database. Every tile is a deep-link into the screen that owns the underlying records, making the dashboard a navigation hub rather than a data dump. Numbers shown must be authorization-scoped to what the signed-in admin may actually see, never a raw global total leaked through a tile.
items.applicability: Use as the default post-login view for any enterprise admin product where operators need an at-a-glance health and workload summary before drilling into specific areas.
items.example: A top row of cards — Projects, Pending Approvals, Imported Files, Active Users — each linking to its list screen, above a 'Recent Imports' table and a service-health strip showing MSSQL Up / Vector Store Optional.
items.limitation: Aggregations computed with group-by-constant projections or by materializing full entities can hang on SQL Server; use separate indexed CountAsync calls and never block on external services.

items.title: User management screen
items.description: The user management screen is a searchable, paged list of accounts showing identity, assigned roles, status (active/disabled/locked), and last-activity, with row actions to enable, disable, or reassign roles. The list must project to a lightweight row record selecting only displayed columns, never the full user entity with large or sensitive blobs. Every mutating action is authorized server-side against the actor's own permissions, and self-destructive actions (an admin disabling their own last admin role) are blocked to prevent lockout. Status is communicated with consistent badges so operators can scan account health quickly.
items.applicability: Use wherever administrators provision, deprovision, or audit human or service accounts and their role assignments.
items.example: A table with columns Username, Display Name, Roles, Status badge, Last Seen, and an actions menu (Edit Roles, Disable, Reset), plus a search box and a status filter.
items.limitation: Disabling or deleting accounts can orphan ownership of records; require explicit reassignment or soft-disable rather than hard delete, and never expose actions the actor lacks rights to perform.

items.title: Role management
items.description: Role management lets administrators define named roles and bind them to discrete permissions, favoring a small set of coarse roles over per-user permission sprawl. The UI presents each role with its permission set as toggles or a checklist, plus a count of how many principals hold it, so the blast radius of a change is visible before it is saved. Built-in system roles (e.g. a primary Administrator) should be protected from deletion or de-privileging, and at least one administrator must always remain. All permission evaluation happens on the server from the persisted role-permission mapping; the client UI is a convenience editor, never the source of truth for what an action is allowed to do.
items.applicability: Use in any product implementing role-based access control where permissions are grouped into reusable roles assigned to users or groups.
items.example: An 'Approver' role page listing permissions (Approve Knowledge, View Audit) as checkboxes, a banner '14 users hold this role', and a disabled Delete button on the protected Administrator role.
items.limitation: Coarse roles can over-grant; if fine-grained needs emerge, add scoped permissions deliberately rather than minting one-off roles per user, which becomes unmanageable.

items.title: Access matrix view
items.description: An access matrix view renders the cross-product of principals (users or roles) against resources (projects, modules, or permissions) as a grid of cells, giving administrators a single auditable picture of who can reach what. It is primarily a read and reconciliation surface: cells use clear allow/deny/inherited indicators, and editing a cell triggers a server-side authorization write rather than a client-only flip. For large estates the matrix must be filtered or virtualized by row and column so it does not attempt to render thousands of cells at once. It is the canonical tool for answering 'who has access to project X' and for spotting over-broad grants during access reviews.
items.applicability: Use during access certification, security reviews, or whenever per-project / per-resource access needs to be inspected and reconciled at a glance.
items.example: Rows = users, columns = projects, with green check (granted), grey dash (none), and a small 'inherited' icon where access flows from a role; filtering to one project collapses the grid to a single column.
items.limitation: A naive full matrix does not scale; always page or filter by resource, and remember the grid only visualizes the server's effective-permission computation — it must never become the authority.

items.title: Import wizard pattern
items.description: The import wizard guides an operator through a multi-step flow — select source, validate/preview, configure mapping, confirm, then track progress — so a long-running ingestion is broken into reviewable stages rather than one opaque upload. Validation and coverage reporting happen before commit, surfacing unsupported files, skipped binaries, and encoding issues so there are no silent blind spots. The actual import is enqueued and processed by a background worker; the wizard's final step shows live status and a completion summary with per-file outcomes instead of blocking the request thread. The pipeline must never crash the UI on a malformed archive — bad files are reported as errors and the batch continues.
items.applicability: Use for any bulk data or document import (ZIP repositories, CSV batches, document sets) where pre-commit validation and asynchronous processing protect the user from partial or failed loads.
items.example: Step 1 upload ZIP, Step 2 'Coverage: 1,240 files, 38 skipped (binary), 4 undecodable' preview, Step 3 confirm, Step 4 a progress bar fed by the IngestionBackgroundService with a final per-file report.
items.limitation: Processing synchronously inside the wizard request will time out on large archives; always enqueue to a background service and poll status, and treat per-file failures as recoverable, not fatal to the batch.

items.title: Audit viewer
items.description: The audit viewer is a read-only, paged, filterable timeline of security- and data-relevant events — who did what, to which entity, when, and from where — backed by an append-only log that the UI cannot edit or delete. Filters by actor, action type, target, and time range let investigators reconstruct a sequence quickly, and each row links to the affected entity where one still exists. The list projects to lightweight rows and is server-side paged because audit tables grow without bound. Crucially, the viewer itself is access-controlled: only roles with an explicit audit-read permission may open it, and viewing is itself an auditable event.
items.applicability: Use anywhere compliance, security investigation, or change traceability requires a tamper-evident record of administrative and data actions.
items.example: Rows like '2026-06-19 14:02 — jdoe approved KnowledgeItem #5821 (project BDM)' with filter chips for Actor, Action, Date range, and a CSV export gated behind permission.
items.limitation: Audit logs must be append-only and retained per policy; never offer edit/delete from the UI, and avoid loading the full table — always page and index on timestamp and actor.

items.title: Configuration screen
items.description: A configuration screen groups system settings into logical sections (connections, optional integrations, model defaults, feature flags) with inline validation, sensible defaults, and a clear save/dirty state. Secrets such as API keys are write-only in the UI — entered once, stored encrypted at rest, and shown as a masked placeholder thereafter, never echoed back. Toggles for optional dependencies should degrade gracefully: disabling a vector store or inference backend must leave the core product fully functional. Saving validates server-side and, where a setting affects security or data integrity, records the change to the audit log.
items.applicability: Use for the settings surface of any admin product that exposes connection strings, integration toggles, secrets, or tunable defaults to operators.
items.example: Sections for 'Database' (read-only effective connection), 'Vector Store (optional)' with an enable toggle and masked API key field, and 'Defaults' with model name and embedding-dimension inputs, all behind one Save button with a dirty indicator.
items.limitation: Never render secrets back to the client or store them in committed config; keep optional-service toggles truly optional so a misconfiguration cannot take core pages down.

items.title: Approval workflow UI
items.description: The approval workflow UI presents a queue of items in a pending state with a multi-select toolbar offering bulk approve, bulk reject, and select-all-across-pages, plus per-item review actions. Each item carries a status badge (Pending / Approved / Rejected) and a transition history, and approval is a server-side state change that records the approver and timestamp to the audit trail. Authorization is enforced on the server for every transition: the UI may hide an Approve button from a non-approver, but the server must independently reject the action, never trusting a hidden or re-enabled control. Select-all-across-pages should resolve to a server-side filter (approve all matching the current query) rather than only the rows loaded on screen.
items.applicability: Use wherever a curated lifecycle gates content into a trusted state — knowledge items, business rules, code snippets, or any submission requiring reviewer sign-off.
items.example: A Pending Knowledge queue with checkboxes, a sticky toolbar reading 'Approve selected (12) | Reject selected', a 'Select all 340 matching' link, and per-row Pending badges that flip to Approved with the approver's name on action.
items.limitation: Client-side hiding of actions is not security; enforce every transition server-side, and ensure bulk select-all approves the filtered set, not just the visible page, to avoid silent partial approvals.

items.title: System health page
items.description: The system health page reports the status of the primary datastore and each optional dependency by reading a cached health snapshot maintained by a background probe, never by calling those services synchronously on the request path. It distinguishes a required component being down (a real outage) from an optional integration being absent or disabled (an expected, non-blocking state), using color and label rather than a single binary. Because the snapshot is cached, the page renders instantly even when an external service is slow or unreachable, preserving the rule that no page may hang on an outside dependency. A last-checked timestamp and the probe interval are shown so operators trust the freshness of what they see.
items.applicability: Use as the operational status surface in any product with a required core store plus optional services that must degrade gracefully when missing.
items.example: Cards: 'SQL Server — Up (checked 12s ago)', 'Vector Store — Optional, disabled', 'Inference — Optional, unreachable', each fed from IServiceHealthCache refreshed by a HealthMonitorService timer.
items.limitation: A synchronous probe inside the controller or view will reintroduce page hangs; always read the cached snapshot, and never let an optional service's state be rendered as a hard failure.

items.title: Knowledge pack management
items.description: Knowledge pack management lists installable packs (curated baseline knowledge bundles) with their version, item count, install state, and review status, and supports import, preview, and selective enable. Import is idempotent and keyed on a stable per-item identifier so re-importing a pack updates in place rather than duplicating, and version is tracked so an admin can see and upgrade what is installed. Imported items enter the same approval lifecycle as authored knowledge — a pack does not bypass curation — and remain attributable to their source pack for later audit or removal. The list projects to lightweight rows (no large content columns) and shows status badges for Installed / Update available / Not installed.
items.applicability: Use where a product ships or accepts curated knowledge bundles that administrators install, version, and fold into an approval-gated memory store.
items.example: A 'professional-base-v1' pack row showing version 1.0.0, 60 items across 6 categories, badge 'Installed', and an Import button that upserts by item UID and routes new items into the pending-approval queue.
items.limitation: Importing must be idempotent and version-aware or packs duplicate on re-run; do not auto-approve pack content or load full item bodies into the management list, and keep source attribution for clean removal.
